perturbation.original_text: 6.2. Subject to the provisions of Article 6.1, the Operating Company shall annually declare and pay by March 15 a distribution to each Party equal to the larger of the two estimated annual tax liabilities as reflected on the approved Party Tax Estimates (the "Minimum Distribution").
perturbation.changed_text: 6.2. Subject to the provisions of Article 6.1, the Operating Company shall annually declare and pay by June 30 a distribution to each Party equal to the larger of the two estimated annual tax liabilities as reflected on the approved Party Tax Estimates (the "Minimum Distribution").
perturbation.explanation: Changing the distribution deadline from March 15 to June 30 could cause inconsistencies if the tax liabilities need to be paid before June 30.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 6072 - Time for filing income tax returns
perturbation.law_explanation: Many businesses file their tax returns well before June 30. Changing the deadline for distribution to June 30 may contradict tax regulations that assume payments related to tax liabilities will be available earlier in the year to meet tax payment deadlines. The exact conflict depends on the specifics of the company's tax obligations and filing choices, but moving the distribution date that late introduces a risk of non-compliance if the parties need the funds to meet earlier tax deadlines.

Time for filing income tax returns (a) General rule In the case of returns under section 6012, 6013, or 6017 (relating to income tax under subtitle A), returns made on the basis of the calendar year shall be filed on or before the 15th day of April following the close of
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a potential legal contradiction. The original contract specified a distribution date of March 15, aligning with typical tax filing deadlines (April 15, as indicated in 26 USC 6072). The changed text moves this deadline to June 30. The Law Explanation notes that businesses often file taxes before June 30, and this shift could cause non-compliance if the funds are needed to meet earlier deadlines. While the scraped snippet does not provide an exact deadline, it references income tax returns filed on or before April 15th, indicating tax obligations may be required before June 30.

perturbation.original_text: 9.3. The Board shall have regular meetings at least once each calendar quarter which shall be called by the Chairman by written notice delivered to each Board member no later than five business days prior to the meeting specifying the date, time and place for such meeting.
perturbation.changed_text: 9.3. The Board shall have regular meetings at least once each calendar quarter which shall be called by the Chairman by written notice delivered to each Board member no later than one business day prior to the meeting specifying the date, time and place for such meeting.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for board meetings to one business day may violate corporate governance standards, especially in jurisdictions requiring longer notice periods for significant decisions.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware General Corporation Law § 222. Notice of Meetings and Adjourned Meetings.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law, commonly governing corporations, requires adequate notice for meetings. While it doesn't specify an exact number of days for all situations, one business day's notice might be deemed insufficient, especially for significant decisions requiring proper deliberation by board members. A short notice could violate the spirit of ensuring informed participation and decision-making, opening the company up to legal challenges. Because the agreement defers to Delaware law, this is a direct contradiction.

General Corporation Law Subchapter VII. Meetings, Elections, Voting and Notice Â§ 211. Meetings of stockholders. (a) (1) Meetings of stockholders may be held at such place, either within or without this State as may be designated by or in the manner provided in the certificate of incorporation or bylaws, or if not so designated, as determined by the board of directors. If, pursuant to this paragraph or the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws of the corporation, the board of directors is authorized to determine the place of a meeting of stockholders, the board of directors may, in its sole discretion, determine that the meeting shall not be held at any place, but may instead be held solely by means of remote communication as authorized by paragraph (a)(2) of this section. (2) If authorized by the board of directors in its sole discretion, and subject to such guidelines and procedures as the board of directors may adopt, stockholders and proxyholders not physically present at a meeting of stockholders may, by means of remote communication: a. Participate in a meeting of stockholders; and b. Be deemed present in person and vote at a meeting of stockholders, whether such meeting is to be held at a designated place or solely by means of remote communication, provided that (i) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to verify that each person deemed present and permitted to vote at the meeting by means of remote communication is a stockholder or proxyholder, (ii) the corporation shall implement reasonable measures to provide such stockholders and proxyholders a reasonable opportunity to participate in the meeting and to vote on matters submitted to the stockholders, including an opportunity to read or hear the proceedings of the meeting substantially concurrently with such proceedings, and (iii) if any stockholder or proxyholder votes or takes o
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 8 Delaware Code § 222 (2023) - Notice of meetings and adjourned meetings. :: 2023 Delaware Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Delaware Code 2023 Delaware Code (here) 2022 Delaware Code View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2023 Delaware Code Title 8 - Corporations Chapter 1. GENERAL CORPORATION LAW Subchapter VII. Meetings, Elections, Voting and Notice § 222. Notice of meetings and adjourned meetings. Universal Citation: 8 DE Code § 222 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 222. Notice of meetings and adjourned meetings. (a) Whenever stockholders are required or permitted to take any action at a meeting, a notice of the meeting shall be given in accordance with § 232 of this title, and such notice shall state the place, if any, date and hour of the meeting, the means of remote communications, if any, by which stockholders and proxy holders may be deemed to be present in person and vote at such meeting, the record date for determining the stockholders entitled to vote at the meeting, if such date is different from the record date for determining stockholders entitled to notice of the meeting, and, in the case of a special meeting, the purpose or purposes for which the meeting is called. (b) Unless otherwise provided in this chapter, the notice of any meeting shall be given not less than 10 nor more than 60 days before the date of the meeting to each stockholder entitled to vote at such meeting as of the record date for determining the stockholders entitled to notice of the meeting. (c) Unless the bylaws otherwise require, when a meeting is adjourned to another time or place, (including an adjournment taken to address a technical failure to convene or continue a meeting using remote communication), notice need not be given of the adjourned meeting if the time, place, if
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the notification period for board meetings from five business days to one, which creates a contradiction with Delaware law. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 8 DE Code § 222 (2023), states that "the notice of any meeting shall be given not less than 10 nor more than 60 days before the date of the meeting." The changed text violates this requirement, as one business day's notice is less than the mandated 10 days.

perturbation.original_text: 12.1. RIGHT OF FIRST REFUSAL; TAG-ALONG RIGHTS. Prior to one year from the Effective Date, a Party may not sell or otherwise transfer its interest in the Operating Company. Thereafter, any sale or other transfer may not be effectuated except pursuant to a good faith, arms' length offer to sell its entire ownership interest in the Operating Company for cash consideration to a party who is not affiliated with such Party (a "Third Party Offer").
perturbation.changed_text: 12.1. RIGHT OF FIRST REFUSAL; TAG-ALONG RIGHTS. Prior to six months from the Effective Date, a Party may not sell or otherwise transfer its interest in the Operating Company. Thereafter, any sale or other transfer may not be effectuated except pursuant to a good faith, arms' length offer to sell its entire ownership interest in the Operating Company for cash consideration to a party who is not affiliated with such Party (a "Third Party Offer").
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the period before a party can sell their interest to six months may be deemed unreasonable restraint on trade or alienation, potentially clashing with common law principles and specific state statutes on business agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 186 states that a promise is unreasonably in restraint of trade if (a) it is not ancillary to an otherwise valid transaction or relationship, or (b) the restraint is greater than needed to protect the promisee's legitimate interest. Reducing the period to six months could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it significantly hinders a party's ability to exit the venture and is not demonstrably necessary to protect the other party's interests. While not a statute, it reflects common law principles applied by many courts.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the period before a party can sell their interest from one year to six months. The Law Explanation states that this reduction 'could be viewed as an unreasonable restraint, particularly if it significantly hinders a party's ability to exit the venture and is not demonstrably necessary to protect the other party's interests', referencing Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 186. Although no law snippets were available, the Law Explanation still provides enough context to determine that the modification creates a legally questionable scenario by potentially creating an unreasonable restraint of trade. This directly contradicts the principles outlined in the Law Explanation.
